State of California

Memorandum

To      :   File                                                    Date    :   12/29/83

                                                                              T- 83-291


From    :   FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
            Jeanne Pritchard

Subject:    Western Growers Association -- Agricultural tour for legislators


            On December 8, 1983, I spoke with Miriam Hubbard and Jasper
Hempel of Western Growers Association about an informational agricul-
tural tour provided to a number of legislative officials by Western
Growers Association.

            The tour was provided on October 11, 1983, and included air
transportation for the officials from their districts to the start of
the tour in Salinas.

            Seven legislators and one AA participated in the tour.  However,
two of the legislators provided their own transportation to the star of
the tour, so the air transportation was provided to only five of the
legislators and the AA.

            Dan Haley, Western Growers' lobbyist, was liaison for Western
Growers, and Rusty Areas was liaison for the legislators.  Haley and
Areas communicated on a number of occasions concerning the travel
arrangements.  Western Growers chartered an airplane to fly three of
the legislators from Sacramento to Salinas.  Western Growers used their
own private plane to fly two of the legislators and the AA from Los
Angeles to Salinas.  Dan Haley was host to the group on the plane from
Sacramento, and Jasper Hempel, also one of Western Growers' lobbyists,
was host to the group on the plane from Los Angeles.

            Although the two lobbyists apparently reliаd that the air
transportation constituted a gift, they did not realize that their
activities -- Dan Haley's communications with Rusty Areas, and Dan
Haley's and Jasper Hempel's meeting the legislators on the plane trips --
constituted "arranging" the gift.

            Mr. Hempel asked what could be done to rectify the violation.  I
suggested that if he requested, and the legislators provided, reimburse-
ment of the cost of the trip, it might minimize the seriousness of the
violation, although it would not rectify it.  I also suggested that
they attach a full explanation and copies of the correspondence to
their quarterly reports.

VVG PAC                                    FPPC

WESTERN GROWERS POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE
P.O. BOX 2130 • NEWPORT BEACH, CA 92658

December 21, 1983


The Honorable Norm Waters
Ms. Susan Reed
California State Assembly
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Norm:

On December 8, 1983, I was advised by Ms. Jean Pritchard,
Political Reform Consultant, Fair Political Practices Commission,
that Dan Haley and myself, as registered lobbyists for the Western
Growers Association, have violated Government Code Section 86203
and Opinion No. 81-003, 7 FPPC 1 (attached) because we aided and
intermediaries by helping to arrange transportation for the Salinas
agricultural tour you participated in on October 11, 1983. By
acting as intermediaries and accompanying you on the plane trip
without another WGA representative on board, Dan and I apparently
arranged travel or provided a gift to you in excess of $10 per
month, which is prohibited by the Act. At the time, neither Dan
nor I was aware that we had committed a technical violation of the
act.

Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests that you
reimburse Western Growers Association $409.00 which represents your
pro rata ½ share of the $858.00 airplane cost, minus our $10.00
gift which is permissible. While this reimbursement will not cure
WGA's technical violation, your reimbursement of that sum may, I
understand, ameliorate a violation on your part.

WGA, Dan Haley and I sincerely apologize for this regrettable
error and for any inconvenience this may cause you. If you have
any questions regarding this matter, please call me at 714/863-1000,
or Ms. Jean Pritchard of the FPPC at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,


JASPER E. HEMPEL
Vice President-Government Affairs

cc: Ms. Jean Pritchard
Mr. Dan Haley

month, which is prohibited by the Act. At the time, neither Dan

act.

Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests that you
reimburse Western Growers Association $204.50 which represents your
pro rata ⅓ share of the $858.00 airplane cost, minus our $10.00 gift
which is permissible. While this reimbursement will not cure WGA's
technical violation, your reimbursement of that sum may, I understand, ameliorate a violation on your part.

WGA, Dan Haley and I sincerely apologize for this regrettable
error and for any inconvenience this may cause you. If you have
any questions regarding this matter, please call me at 714/863-1000,
or Ms. Jean Pritchard of the FPPC at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,

JASPER E. HEMPEL

     Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests that you reimburse

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660            322-5901            322-6441              322-6444

December 15, 1983

Jasper Hempel
Western Growers Association
P.O. Box 2130
Newport Beach, CA  92658

Dear Mr. Hempel:

     Enclosed please find FPPC Opinion No. 81-003 (Institute
for Governmental Advocates, 7 FPPC 1), which discusses the
concept of "arranging" as it applies to lobbyists involved in
making gifts to state officials.

     Although the circumstances addressed by the opinion are
quite different from the situation we discussed on the telephone,
the general discussion of "arranging" will provide guidance in
defining prohibited activities of a lobbyist in connection with
making gifts to officials.  Specifically, it is stated in the
analysis, on page 5, that:

     A lobbyist is acting as an agent in, or arranging
for, the making of a gift by another when he or she:

1.    Takes any action involving contact with
      a third party which facilitates the making
      of the gift.

2.    Has any contact with the public official
      who is to be the recipient of the gift
      which facilitates the making of the gift.

     With regard to the situation we discussed on the telephone,
please note that Government Code Section 82028(b)(1) excludes
"informational material" from the definition of "gift"; however,
that section provides that, "No payment for travel or reimburse-
ment for any expenses shall be deemed 'informational material.'"

     Please call me if you have any questions.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Jeanne Pritchard
Political Reform Consultant

cc: Miriam Hubbard
     Western Growers Association

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

State of California
DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

350 McALLISTER STREET, ROOM 6000
SAN FRANCISCO 94102
(415) 557-2544

(415) 557-1586

November 21, 1983

Barbara Milman
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

Opinion No. 83-1008

Enclosed is an opinion request and attendant materials we have received from the County Counsel of Yuba County presenting conflict of interest questions with respect to a director of the Yuba County Water Agency who is also a director of a California Water District and a landowner within the latter district.

In accordance with our policy to request views of interested parties or associations, you are invited to submit whatever views you may have on the questions presented.

Very truly yours,

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

[signature]
CLAYTON P. ROCHE
Deputy Attorney General

YUBA COUNTY WATER AGENCY

MINUTES - REGULAR MEETING - BOARD OF DIRECTORS

SEPTEMBER 25, 1979

A regular meeting of the Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency was
held on Tuesday, September 25, 1979, commencing at 9:00 a.m. in the Supervisors
Chambers, Yuba County Courthouse, Marysville, California. Present were Directors
George Deveraux, Harold J. Sperbeck, Douglas Waltz and Charlie Parker. Director
Jim Pharris was not in attendance. Also present were Administrator Donald Frost,
General Counsel Dennis Barlow and Deputy Clerk Patricia Stewart. Chairman Waltz
presided.

APPROVAL OF CLAIMS

Upon motion of Director Sperbeck, seconded by Director Parker and unanimously
carried, with Director Pharris being absent, General Fund Claims in the total
amount of $5,049.45 were approved.

APPROVE GE
FUND CLAIM

Following the vote on the motion Mr. Frost commented that the amounts listed pro-
vided for the draw-down of the $12,000 to each District (Wheatland Water District
and Brophy Water District), which was the subject of an agreement between the
ency Board of Directors and the Districts. It was anticipated, from what Mr.
St. Maurice had indicated, the Districts would be requesting supplemental funding
from the Board. He had been advised by Mr. Frost that such a request must be di-
rected to the Board.

Mr. Frost further commented that in making these advances to the Districts, the
Board should take into consideration and perhaps ask clarification from the Dis-
tricts as to how much money should be advanced to the Districts for initial en-
gineering and review of engineering already paid for by this Board. It was clear
to Mr. Frost that this must be clarified before the final design stage.

Chairman Waltz suggested that Mr. Frost meet with the two engineering firms and
representatives of the Districts to work out a schedule as to who is going to do
what before they ask for any additional funds. There were no objections to the
proposal and it was so ordered.

EXECUTION OF MARINA CONTRACT

Mr. Frost noted that the contract had been executed by Mr. Cray. There were no
changes from the document previously submitted to the Board.

Upon motion of Director Sperbeck, seconded by Director Deveraux and unanimously
carried, with Director Pharris being absent, the Board approved and authorized
the Chairman to execute CONTRACT FOR THE CONSTRUCTION AND OPERATION OF A MARINA
AT NEW BULLARDS BAR RESERVOIR BY AND BETWEEN YUBA WATER AGENCY AND JERRY CRAY.

APPROVE CRA
MARINA AGMT

../25/79                                    - 1 -

ALLOCATION OF WATER TO SOUTH YUBA

Mr. Frost made reference to a letter from the proposed South Yuba Water District,
copies of which had been distributed to Board members at the special meeting the
previous evening. He noted there were also letters from the Wheatland Water Dis-
trict and Brophy Water District.

The Advisory Council and the committees of the two Districts, as well as the pro-
posed district, met twice. The end result of those discussions was articulated
in the two letters from the Districts. In effect, Brophy and Wheatland felt that
the available water should be allocated on the basis of irrigable acreage, in the
initial areas to be served do not include the entire irrigable area within a dis-
trict, that district which has the responsibility of paying for the water should
have the discretion of allocating surplus water within that district. The Brophy
Yuba proposal recommended that the water not allocated on a service area basis be
pooled for use by whatever users may be able to utilize it effectively. It was
noted that other ways of apportioning the water were also possible.

It was the staff recommendation that the Board use either the gross area or
irrigable area, rather than the service area, and use the same standard for all
three districts. Further, the irrigable area or usable land seemed to staff to
be the most desirable. [illegible]

Chairman Waltz noted that 1,500 acre feet had been asked for by the City of Wheat-
land. Mr. Frost commented that would be an additional amount, as well as 2,700
for Olivehurst and 5,000 for Linda.

Chairman Waltz wondered if the Board could allocate water to a district new pre-
sently in existence. Mr. Frost responded that they could not contract for the
sale of it, but he knew no reason that says the Board would have to contract for
all the water in the river at any one time. However, by the first of the year the
Board would need an indication of where the water was to go. He believed the Board
would only allocate the amount of water to the districts that are formed and hold
the other amount until that district was formed. Mr. Barlow concurred.

The actual formation of the South Yuba Water District was briefly discussed. Mr.
Frost noted that if their formation took the normal three months, they would be
able to tell the Board by the end of the year whether or not there will be a con-
tract.

Director Parker noted that with the construction of the Marysville Dam there would
be extra storage capacity and he believed more water. It was his desire to see
the protection of other districts if there was more water available to the South
Yuba Canal. He made reference to correspondence which he had received from the
Yuba County Water District. In order to avoid a lawsuit he felt they should be
negotiated with and the Board should give definite guidelines as to what they
could or could not do. Mr. Frost was of the belief that the actions which would
be critical to the water district letter would be execution of contracts with
Brophy and Wheatland. The action this date, although it would set a course of

5/25/79                                - 2 -

action that could lead to a contract, was not execution of a contract.

During the course of discussion of this matter comments and/or questions were received from the following persons in the audience: Mike Rue; Charles de St. Maurice; Robert Blackford; Art Bertolini; Price Waltz.

Chairman Waltz commented that because he was a land owner within the Wheatland Water District and a potential user of the water, upon advice of counsel, he would abstain from voting on this matter. He then stated his opinion that irrigable acreage allocation was the most equitable for all involved. He felt this board was in the position of telling each water district how they should allocate their water.

Motion was offered by Director Parker, seconded by Director Deveraux, that the Board accept the following proposal: Acre feet apportioned by irrigable area - of irrigable Wheatland, 42,100; Brophy, 32,000; South Tuba, 20,900. Motion passed, with Chairman Waltz abstaining and with Director Pharris being absent.

REGULATION CHARGE

Chairman Waltz noted that the regulation charge was set at $1.00 per acre foot, effective March 1982 (refer to meeting of September 24, 1979).

1978-79 AUDIT REPORT

Mr. Frost noted that subject report had been prepared as required and copies had been distributed. No action required.

CORRESPONDENCE

1. Letter from City of Wheatland dated September 13, 1979, reaffirming their request for 1,500 acre feet of water from the Yuba County Water Agency. No action required at this time, per Chairman Waltz.

2. Letter from Local Union 1245, International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers, dated September 1, 1979, indicating their desire to meet and confer with representatives of the Agency regarding salaries, fringe benefits, hours of work and other terms and conditions of employ[illegible] for those employees of the Agency represented by the union. There being no objections, Chairman Waltz directed staff to meet with IBEW.

OTHER BUSINESS

Chairman Waltz noted that at the recent Sacramento-Mother Lode convention there was a presentation by the California Conservation Corps regarding the types of projects they do. He requested that Mr. Frost contact them to determine if there was any possibility of getting one of their crews to work on clearing the reservoir. There were no objections.

5/79                                    - 3 -

The meeting was adjourned at 9:32 a.m.

________________________                    ________________________
George Deveraux, Secretary                  Douglas Wartz, Chairman

________________________
Patricia H. Stewart, Deputy Clerk

Approved: November 27, 1979

- 4 -

YUBA COUNTY WATER AGENCY

MINUTES - SPECIAL MEETING - BOARD OF DIRECTORS

SEPTEMBER 24, 1979

A special meeting of the Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency was
held on Monday, September 24, 1979, commencing at 7:30 p.m. at the John Packard
Library, Marysville, California. Present were Directors George Deveraux, Harold J.
Sperbeck, Douglas Waltz and Charlie Parker, who was delayed in arriving. Director
Jim Harris was not in attendance. Also present were Administrator Donald Frost
and Deputy Clerk Patricia Stewart. Chairman Waltz presided.

JOINT MEETING WITH ADVISORY COUNCIL

Amount of Payment to Yuba County Water Agency for Agency Furnished Water: The fol-
lowing Advisory Council representatives were in attendance: Robert H. Blackford
and Price Waltz, Wheatland Water District; Arthur Bertolini, Jess Cantrell and H.
Earl Parker, Brophy Water District; Herbert W. Greydanus, Wheatland and Brophy
Water Districts; Jack O'Keefe and Harold A. Johnson, Yuba County Water District;
Sam Shindaffer and Lloyd Ruh, Hallwood Irrigation Company; Dave DeBlsick, Oive-
hurst Public Utility District; Carlin Hofman, Michael Rue and Frances E. Hofman,
posed South Yuba Water District; Charles Mathews, Cordua Irrigation District.
Also present were Jim Earl, Engineer for CH2M Hill, and Charles de St. Maurice.

During the course of the meeting the following correspondence, containing various
proposals regarding the sale of water, was presented: (a) letter (undated) from
Cordua Irrigation District; (b) letter dated September 24, 1979, from proposed
South Yuba Water District; (c) letter dated September 10, 1979, from Wheatland
Water District. Mr. Frost's comments on the rate were contained in his letter
dated September 20, 1979.

Mr. Frost presented copies of Consumers Price Index California Composite, which he
suggested would provide a rational method of measuring price increases: He noted
that existing Agency contracts provided that the cost could be increased after
ten years from the 50% rate. Reference was made to an excerpt from an existing
contract relative to both increases and decreases in the rate. He felt that in-
dicated the Agency had followed a policy of charging irrigators related costs.

Mr. Frost then explained the formula by which the June, 1979, price of 88¢ per
acre foot was derived. Projected ahead to 1982, using March to March as a base,
the rate for the period March to June would be $1.02. It was his opinion that a
50% base in 1972, using the Consumers Price Index California Composite, the price
would be "around" $1.00 by 1982.

It was noted by Mr. Frost that existing contracts have a ten-year clause. He felt
that was appropriate in the more stable economic period of the 1950's. His opera-
tional opinion was that a five-year period would be more appropriate for review in
light of today's economy.

9/24/79                                  - 1 -

Throughout the meeting questions and/or comments were directed to the Board by
various Advisory Council representatives listed above.

Chairman Waltz commented that he had inquired of the Agency's Council as to a pos-
sible conflict of interest inasmuch as he was a land owner in the area involved.
Upon advice of Counsel, Chairman Waltz stated he would abstain from acting upon this
matter.

In response to a question raised by Mr. Blackford, Mr. Frost stated that the Con-
sumers Price Index was selected because it was generally known. It was also an in-
dicator of what the Agency's costs would be at a time and what they would have to
pay for utilities, help, engineering, services, legal services, etc. To that ex-
tent it was a valid measure of the Agency's operating costs. However, if there
were some other standards or index that would be better than the CPI, he felt sure
the Board would want to hear about it.

Director Parker arrived at this point in the meeting, at 8:20 p.m. The price for-
mula, which had been discussed prior to his arrival, was reviewed.

BWC Earls indicated it was anticipated the water would be available for use by the
farmers in 1983.

Motion was offered by Director Sperbeck, seconded by Director Deveraux, that the MOTION RE
five-year review and the cost of living index (Consumers Price Index California FORMULA FOR
Composite) be the basis for consideration in setting $1.00 per acre foot as of WATER COST
March, 1982, with the CPI being used as the inflation factor. Motion passed
with Chairman Waltz abstaining and with Director Parris being absent.

There followed a brief discussion relative to formation of the proposed South
Yuba Water District. Mr. Waltz noted that the group would not be a legal entity
until approximately March or May, 1980, based on their present schedule.

Mr. Mathews inquired if all the lands in South Yuba County would have access to
the water at some point in the future. Chairman Waltz was of the belief that the
people who applied for it at this time would be the ones who get the water.

The meeting was adjourned at 8:50 p.m.

_______________________          _______________________
George Deveraux, Secretary        Douglas Waltz, Chairman

_______________________          Approved:  November 27, 1979
Patricia H. Stewart, Deputy Clerk

9/24/79                                    - 2 -

OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL

DENNIS A. BARLOW

COURTHOUSE - 3rd Floor
215 - Fifth Street
Marysville, CA 95901

COUNTY COUNSEL

(916) 741-6401

TIMOTHY P. HAYES
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

October 18, 1983

The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp
Attorney General
1515 K Street, Suite 511
Sacramento, CA 95814

ATTN: JACK R. WINKLER, ASSISTANT ATTORNEY GENERAL
CHIEF, OPINION UNIT

Re: Opinion Request

Dear Mr. Van De Kamp:

On September 27 and October 12 I submitted an opinion request on
the following questions related to the Yuba County Water Agency:

1. May a director of the Yuba County Water Agency who is also
a director of a California Water District lawfully act on matters
which directly affect the water district?

2. May a director of the Yuba County Water Agency who owns
land within a California Water District lawfully participate in
Agency actions which will provide water to such district?

I have attached copies of our correspondence in this matter.

You have asked for additional clarification on these questions.

Pursuant to the authority of §5.1(c) and (d) of the Agency Act (see
Water Code--Appendix §84-5.1(c) and (d)), the Water Agency sells
water to the member districts. In addition the Agency has the power
to construct water storage, flood control and power generation
facilities and has done so in the construction of the New Bullards
Bar Dam. In addition the Agency has over the past several years
planned for the construction of a canal taking water from the Yuba
River to the southern part of the County for sale to the three
water districts organized in that area. Although recently two of

The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp
ATTN: JACK R. WINKLER, Assistant Attorney General
October 18, 1983
Page 2

the three districts have apparently withdrawn from the joint
project, Wheatland Water District is still interested and all
three are interested in entering into water contracts with the
Agency.

The above are the types of "matters which directly affect" the
Water District to which my letter of September 27 referred.

In reference to the second question noted above I have advised
the Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency on several
occasions in relation to conflicts of interest and my letter to
them dated May 16, 1983 was merely a summary of those previous
opinions.

The basic provision of the Political Reform Act of 1974 provides
as follows:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

"Public official" as used in this section means "every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency."
(Government Code §82048) and "local government agency" would certainly
include the Yuba County Water Agency (See Government Code §82041).

In the present question the member of the Board of Directors is a
rice grower and owner of substantial property in the Wheatland
Water District. The actions of the Agency in securing a loan for
a canal system and then selling the water to the district which
would in turn develop a distribution system and sell the water to
the individual landowners in the district would be of benefit to
him in that his costs in securing water would be decreased, however
the cases have concluded that such a public official is not required
to disqualify himself from participation in such decisions if the
benefit to him will be the same as that to a significant segment of
the public (see Metropolitan Water Dist. of Southern California v.
Fair Political Practices Commission (1977) 73 CA3d 650). In this
instance the Director will receive the same benefit as nearly all
the other landowners in the Wheatland Water District and therefore
is not disqualified from participating in the questioned decisions.

You also raised a question as to the applicability of Government
Code §1126 to this question. This section as well as Government
Code §1090 et seq. relate primarily to incompatible employment of
those in public service. This situation is not raised in the pre-
sent fact situation. Here the question is whether or not the
Director is receiving a private benefit from a public decision.

The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp
ATTN:  JACK R. WINKLER, Assistant Attorney General
October 18, 1983
Page 3

In addition §7.1 of the Agency Act (see Water Code--Appendix
§84-7.1) prohibits a director from being "interested in any manner,
directly or indirectly, in any contract awarded or to be awarded
by the Board of Directors, or in the profits to be derived therefrom."
There is no statutory guidance in construing this section and no
reported cases.  However there is no indication that the intent of
this section is more restrictive than the provisions of the Political
Reform Act of 1974 discussed above.

It has been and remains the conclusion of this office that a member
of the Board of Supervisors sitting ex officio as a member of the
Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency is not disqualified
from participating in decisions of the Board of Directors in planning
and constructing water conveyance facilities to provide water to the
Wheatland Water District by the fact that that Supervisor is also a
landowner in the district, and would benefit along with others by
the receipt of such water.

I appreciate your attention to the above noted questions.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
DENNIS A. BARLOW
County Counsel

DAB:eoh

OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL

DENNIS A. BARLOW                                                    COURTHOUSE - 3rd Floor
COUNTY COUNSEL                                                      215 - Fifth Street
                                                                    Marysville, CA 95901
TIMOTHY P. HAYES
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL                                        (916) 741-6401

                                    [YUBA COUNTY SEAL]

                                    October 12, 1983


The Honorable John Van de Kamp
Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA  95814

     Re:  Opinion Request - Yuba County Water Agency

Dear Mr. Van de Kamp:

     On September 27, 1983 I wrote to you with a request for an opinion
as to whether there was a Conflict of Interest for a Director of
the Yuba County Water Agency who also served as a director of an
individual water district to act on matters which directly affected
the Water District.  (A copy of the opinion request is attached).

     The Board of Directors has now asked that I expand my request to
include whether a member of the Board of Directors who is also
a member of the Board of Supervisors and a landowner in a California
Water District located in the County has a conflict of interest if
he participates in decisions of the Water Agency related to a
project to bring water to the district.  The opinion of this office
on this question is contained in the last portion of our memorandum
to the Board of Directors of the Yuba County Water Agency dated
May 16, 1983 attached to our original opinion request.

     Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        DENNIS A. BARLOW
                                        County Counsel

DAB:eoh

enc.                                                                RECEIVED
                                                                    
                                                                    OPINION UNIT

OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL

DENNIS A. BARLOW

COURTHOUSE - 3rd Floor
215 - Fifth Street
Marysville, CA 95901

COUNTY COUNSEL

(916) 674-6401

TIMOTHY P. HAYES
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

September 27, 1983

The Honorable John Van de Kamp
Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re: Opinion Request - Yuba County Water Agency

Dear Mr. Van de Kamp:

In my capacity as General Counsel to the Yuba County Water Agency
I hereby submit a request for an opinion on the following subject.

The Yuba County Water Agency was established by Chapter 788 of the
Statutes of 1959 (see now Chapter 84 of the Water Code--Appendix).
Section 7 of the original Agency Act provided that the Board of
Supervisors of Yuba County should serve, ex officio, as the Board
of Directors of the Agency.  (Water Code--Appendix: 84-7).  However,
this section was amended by Chapter 719 of the Statutes of 1979 to
provide that in addition to the Board of Supervisors, the Agency
Board shall also include two members of the advisory council, one
to represent the districts north of the Yuba River and one to
represent the districts south of the Yuba River, in staggered
two year terms.

The Advisory Council is made up of officers of the various water
related districts and the two cities in the County.  Therefore,
the two additional directors are also officers of such districts.
The question has arisen whether such an Agency director can act
on matters which directly effect the district on which he
also serves.  Attached is an opinion of this office dated May 16,
1983, the first part of which is directed to this question.

RECEIVED

OPINION UNIT

The Honorable John Van de Kamp
September 27, 1983
Page 2

I would appreciate an opinion from your office on this question.
Please feel free to contact my office on this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
DENNIS A. BARLOW
County Counsel

DAB:ech

It is clear that under this common law doctrine the office of 
California Water District Director and Yuba County Water Agency 
Director would be incompatible. 
Therefore acceptance of the 
office of Agency Direetor would create a vacancy in the previously 
held office of District Director. 
However this common law rule has been superceded by specific

the Water Code Appendix) as amended by 11 of Chapter 719 of the 
Statutes of 1979 provides that the Yuba County Water Agency 
Advisory Council shall select two members of the Board of Directors 
to serve for alternate two year terms. 
One to represent the districts 
to the north of the Yuba River and one to represent those south of 
the river.

TIMOTHY P. HAYES

OFFICE OF 
COUNTY COUNSE L 
October 27, 1983 
The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp 
Attorney General 
1515 K Street, Suite 511 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
ATTN: 
JACK R. WINKLER, Assistant Attorney General 
Chief, Opinion Unit 
Dear Mr. Van De Kamp: 
COU RTHOUSE • 3rd Floor 
215 . Fifth Stree~ 
Marysville, CA 95901 
(916) 741·6401 
On September 27 and October 12 I submitted an opinion request, with 
a clarifying letter sent on October 18, 1983. 
I am now in receipt 
of a copy of a letter to you from Mr. Paul Minasian, the attorney 
for South Yuba Water District, attempting to provide additional
sections.facts:
information. 
He refers to the PL 984 project and notes the various districts 
that anticipated receipt of such water. 
It should be understood 
that the amount of water to be delivered by the PL 984 project 
was approximately 107,200 acre feet with only 12,200 acre feet 
of that intended for the TTlunicipal end industrie.l uses. 
This 
amount would have a minimal affect on the property owners in the 
districts affected. 
The members of the Board living in Linda, 
Olivehurst and the City of Marysville, although landowners, are 
certainly not affected by a water project in the same way that an 
agricultural landowner would be. 
It should also be noted that if it were a conflict of interest for 
a Director on the Agency Board who is also a director of a member 
district to act on a water contract or project that may limit or 
affect his district's future water rights, the two district repre-
sentatives on the Board would almost never be able to participate 
in an action of the Board. 
Certainly, that was not the intent of 
the legislature in adopting the revision to §7 of the Agency Act. 


The Honorable John K. Van De Kamp 
October 27, 1983  
If this office can provide any assistance please contact us. 
DAB:eoh

Paul Minasian, Attorney at Law 
Very. truly yO~

JOHN C ... RI..ETON GI'!"'Y, 1872.U13

"''''UI.. JACKSON MINASIAN, 11133.11181 
You have recently apparently received a letter from the 
Yuba County Water Agency making inquiries in regard to your 
opinion in respect to a potential conflict of interest on 
the Yuba County Water Agency Board. 
That request for an 
opinion was initiated at the request of three of the seven 
directors who contacted the County Counsel immediately after 
a vote in which those three directors were overruled by the 
majority of four. 
At the following meeting of the Yuba 
County Water Agency, it became clear that the request had 
been forwarded to the Attorney General's Office at the 
request of the minority. 
At that meeting, on October 11, 
1983, the Board of the Yuba County Water Agency specifically 
asked that the request to the Attorney General be expanded 
to deal with potential conflicts on the part of the other 
members of the Board. 
You should by this time have received 
that request. 
We wish to make it clear that by mentioning and describing 
other Board members in this letter, we are not inferring 
that any conflict of interest should be declared by them 
except in regard to Doug Waltz in certain circumstances. 
To understand the inquiries, you will need some factual 
background and it is our purpose in writing to 
directly 
to provide that factual background. 
Obviously, if there is 
any difference between our understanding and that of Mr. 
Barlow, Yuba County Counsel, Mr. Barlow should have an 
opportunity to correct it but we are taking this initiative 
so that this opinion can be obtained as quickly as possible. 
I 
' 
C 
I' 


Page -2-
Opinion Division 
October 26, 1983 
Yuba County Water Agency consists of five members of 
the Board of Supervisors, and two representatives of the 
member units of the Agency who are elected by those member 
units. 
In reviewing Section 7 of the Act you can see the 
procedure and the qualifications required. 
The Advisory 
Counsel has adopted rules and regulations. 
Those rules and 
regulations provide that the only person who may serve upon 
the Advisory Counsel are members of the boards of the various 
member units, and that the only persons who may be elected 
to the Agency Board from the Advisory Counsel shall be 
members of the boards of the various member units. 
Therefore, 
it is a qualification for the two representatives, not 
members of the Board of Supervisors, that they be members of 
the Board of Directors of a member unit, and that they be 
elected by the Advisory Counsel consisting only of members 
of the Board of Directors of member units. 
The particular range of issues in which the conflict of 
interest question arises will be important for you 
to know. 
The Yuba County Water Agency has proposed a water distribution 
project to divert waters from the Yuba River for use within 
the South County Area. 
There are three irrigation districts, 
South Yuba Water District, Wheatland Water District, Brophy 
Water District, and two municipal and industrial water 
users, Olivehurst Public Utility District and Linda County 
Water District, and one city, the City of Wheatland, who 
would be served by this project. 
The project is to be 
financed by a PL984 Federal Loan. 
South Yuba Water District and Brophy Water 
strict 
have recently notified the Agency that they believe the 
PL984 Project is too expens 
and does not provide sufficient 
benefits, and have requested that their share of the Agency 
water already allocated by the Agency be provided to these 
Districts by a water purchase Agreement in the Yuba River 
regardless of whether or not the PL984 Project is ever 
constructed. 
It would then be South Yuba 
obligation to construct their own distribut 
This notification has 
Wheatland Water 
District and the landowners within that District who were to 
be substantially subsidized by Brophy and South Yuba under 
the PL984 plan. 
The Wheatland Water District has been told 
that the amount of water originally allocated to them by the 
Yuba County Water Agency approximately 42,000 acre feet, is 
insufficient to 
cal 
provide for even a PL984 loan 
water di 
but 
project (a loan without interest, as to 


Page -3-
Opinion Division 
October 26, 1983 
water served to lands eligible under the 160 acre limitation 
law) unless at least 65,000 acre feet can be provided. 
Mr. 
Doug Waltz, a supervisor from the area, and a landowner 
within the Wheatland Water District, and a brother of one of 
the Wheatland Water District directors Mr. Price Waltz, has 
advised and encouraged the Wheatland Water District to place 
upon the ballot an election and debt authorization which is 
based upon 65,000 acre feet of water being contracted and 
available to the Wheatland Water District. 
This issue has 
never however come up to a vote in the Yuba County Water 
Agency as to whether or not water should be taken from 
Brophy and South Yuba and allocated to Wheatland. 
At the 
same time the Brophy and South Yuba Water Districts are 
asking for their contract for the amounts of water based 
upon this apportionment made some years ago. 
The base information that you will need regarding the 
membership of the Agency and their potential conflicts arise 
from the following: 
1. 
Michael Rue is a Director and President of the 
Board of South Yuba Water District, elected by the advisory 
agency to the Yuba County Water Agency. 
He is of course a 
landowner within South Yuba Water District. 
2. 
Doug Waltz, Supervisor in Yuba County, and also a 
landowner wi thin the VJhea tland Water District. 
r1r. Waltz is 
the brother of a director of the Wheatland Water District, 
Price Waltz. 
3. 
Sam Shintaffer, a landowner within the Hallwood 
Water Company, a member unit holding a contract for waters 
from the Yuba River which lies north of the River. 
The 
amount or quantity of waters which are contracted for by the 
Yuba County Water Agency south of Yuba River may in the 
future have some impact upon the ability of Hallwood Water 
Company to purchase water under a clause in their water 
contract permitting them to so purchase and to the application 
of deficiency clauses during dry periods. 
The application 
of tho 
deficiency clauses will probably be more strict and 
occur more often after contracts are entered 
for water 
service in the South County area. 
4. 
Charles Center, a member of the Board of Supervisors, 
who lives and owns property within the boundaries of the 
Olivehurst Public Utility District. 
The costs of water to 
the Olivehurst Public Utility District, Linda County Water 
District, South Yuba, Brophy or Wheatland or the City of 


Page -4-
Opinion Division 
October 26, 1983 
Wheatland, would all vary to some degree with the question 
of whether or not South Yuba and Brophy are denied a water 
contract from the river or whether they are required to 
participate in the PL984 project. 
Further, the question of 
allocation of amounts of water among these districts will 
have a dramatic affect upon the costs of their water service. 
5. 
John Dower, Supervisor from the Marysville area, is 
a landowner within the City of Marysville. 
The City buys 
water under a contract with the Agency for recreational 
filling of a lake. 
6. 
Mr. McGill is a Supervisor, and a resident landowner 
within the Yuba County Water District, a California water 
district within Yuba County. 
This District does not presently 
have contract for water but has been allocated a large 
amount of water by the Agency under its minutes in the past. 
Any water contract entered into in the South County area 
affects the chances that the Agency will live up to its 
original premises or will apply the deficiency clause on a 
strict basis should a contract be entered into hereafter 
with Yuba County Water District. 
7. 
Mr. Devereaux is the Supervisor from the Linda area 
and we are informed that he lives and owns land within the 
boundaries of the Linda County Water Agency. 
Under the 
PL984 project, this District like the Olivehurst Public 
Utility District would obtain substantial amounts of water 
with no financial contribution or guarantees until those 
agencies elected to take water from the project. 
In 1979, through the attached minutes, you will see 
that the County Counsel dealt with the specific question of 
conflict of interest arising from Supervisor Waltz voting 
upon an allocation of water to the Wheatland Water District. 
At that time, Mr. Rue and Mr. Shintaffer were not upon the 
Board of the Agency. 
The particular areas or votes which cou 
come up which 
you should consider from a point of view of a conflict of 
interest are as follows: 
1. 
A motion to reallocate water among the South County 
entities. 
2. 
The voting upon particularly contractual provisions 
to be placed in the contracts of the South County entit s. 
Under the terms of the Yuba County Water Agency Act, any 
more beneficial term placed in a member unit contract for 


Page -5-
Opinion Division 
October 26, 1983 
the South County area is required to be included in any 
existing member unit contract north of the river pursuant to 
Section 6 of the Act which states in part: 
NNo contract may be entered into 
between the agency and a member 
unit which provides for the sale 
of project water for agricultural 
purposes, industrial purposes, ... 
upon any term or condition more 
favorable than the same term or 
condition contained in the agri-
cultural water contracts entered 
into between the agency and member 
units prior to January 1, 1978." 
3. 
The next area of vote that might come up would be 
votes relating to the allocation of assets, water, money, or 
staff effort in favor of the Wheatland Water District 
continuance of the PL984 project, or any attempt to collect 
any portion of the costs of the PL984 project incurred to 
date from Brophy and South Yuba or the use of the credit or 
financing of the Yuba County Water Agency for the benefit of 
the Wheatland Water District. 
We hope that the above description of the factual 
background which we are assured Mr. Barlow will confirm, 
will be of aide to you in your investigation of this matter. 
Our view is that except upon specific votes to allocate 
more or less water to particular distr 
in which a landowner 
member of the Agency Board may own land, that no conflict of 
interest exists. 
Mr. Barlow, in 1979, specifically ruled 
that Mr. Waltz had a conflict of interest in regard to any 
vote or action which directly or indirectly would allocate 
water to one or the other of the member units. 
Mr. Rue 
would seem to have the same conflict now, and if any change 
is proposed in regard to the allocation to Linda and Olivehurst 
District perhaps Mr. Center and Mr. Devereaux should withdraw. 
We agree with Mr. Barlow's previous analysis and look forward 
to your opinion. 


Page -6-
Opinion Division 
October 26, 1983

sections.analysis:
, on page 5, that: 
A lobbyist is acting as an agent in, or arranging 
for, the making of a gift by another when he or she: 
1. 
Takes any action involving contact with 
a third party which facilitates the making 
of the gift. 
2. 
Has any contact with the public official 
who is to be the recipient of the gift 
which facilitates the making of the gift. 
With regard to the situation we discussed on the telephone, 
please note that Government Code Section 82028(b) (1) excludes 
"informational material" from the definition of "gift"; however, 
that section provides that, "No payment for travel or reimburse-
ment for any expenses shall be deemed 'informational material. '" 
Please call me if you have any questions.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Western Growers Association lobbyists violated gift and intermediary provisions of the Political Reform Act by arranging and hosting legislative officials on subsidized air transportation to an agricultural tour.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that the lobbyists violated Government Code Section 86203 by acting as intermediaries to arrange travel gifts exceeding $10 per month to state officials, and recommended reimbursement and disclosure on quarterly reports to ameliorate the violation.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Mixed document set including: (1) FPPC internal memorandum from Jeanne Pritchard to file documenting phone consultation, (2) two separate letters from Jasper Hempel requesting reimbursement from legislators, (3) FPPC response letter to Hempel with Opinion 81-003 reference, (4) Attorney General transmittal letter and unrelated Yuba County Water Agency minutes. Primary enforcement matter is clear; some peripheral documents included; OCR quality affects confidence slightly.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC determined that the lobbyists violated Government Code Section 86203 by acting as intermediaries to arrange travel gifts exceeding $10 per month to state officials, and recommended reimbursement and disclosure on quarterly reports to ameliorate the violation.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : 12/29/83 T- 83-291 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Jeanne Pritchard Subject: Western Growers Association -- Agricultural tour for legislators On December 8, 1983, I spoke with Miriam Hubbard and Jasper Hempel of Western Growers Association about an informational agricul- tural tour provided to a number of legislative officials by Western Growers Association. The tour was provided on October 11, 1983, and included air transportation for the officials from their districts to the start of the tour in Salinas. Seven legislators and one AA participated in the tour. However, two of the legislators provided their own transportation to the star of the tour, so the air transportation was provided to only five of the legislators and the AA. Dan Haley, Western Growers' lobbyist, was liaison for Western Growers, and Rusty Areas was liaison for the legislators. Haley and Areas communicated on a number of occasions concerning the travel arrangements. Western Growers chartered an airplane to fly three of the legislators from Sacramento to Salinas. Western Growers used their own private plane to fly two of the legislators and the AA from Los Angeles to Salinas. Dan Haley was host to the group on the plane from Sacramento, and Jasper Hempel, also one of Western Growers' lobbyists, was host to the group on the plane from Los Angeles. Although the two lobbyists apparently reliаd that the air transportation constituted a gift, they did not realize that their activities -- Dan Haley's communications with Rusty Areas, and Dan Haley's and Jasper Hempel's meeting the legislators on the plane trips -- constituted "arranging" the gift. Mr. Hempel asked what could be done to rectify the violation. I suggested that if he requested, and the legislators provided, reimburse- ment of the cost of the trip, it might minimize the seriousness of the violation, although it would not rectify it. I also suggested that they attach a full explanation and copies of the correspondence to their quarterly reports. VVG PAC FPPC WESTERN GROWERS POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE P.O. BOX 2130 • NEWPORT BEACH, CA 92658 December 21, 1983 The Honorable Norm Waters Ms. Susan Reed California State Assembly State Capitol Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Norm: On December 8, 1983, I was advised by Ms. Jean Pritchard, Political Reform Consultant, Fair Political Practices Commission, that Dan Haley and myself, as registered lobbyists for the Western Growers Association, have violated Government Code Section 86203 and Opinion No. 81-003, 7 FPPC 1 (attached) because we aided and intermediaries by helping to arrange transportation for the Salinas agricultural tour you participated in on October 11, 1983. By acting as intermediaries and accompanying you on the plane trip without another WGA representative on board, Dan and I apparently arranged travel or provided a gift to you in excess of $10 per month, which is prohibited by the Act. At the time, neither Dan nor I was aware that we had committed a technical violation of the act. Nevertheless, Western Growers Association requests
embedding.summary: This document package contains FPPC correspondence and enforcement communications regarding a December 1983 incident where Western Growers Association lobbyists arranged subsidized air transportation for seven legislators and one aide to attend an agricultural tour in Salinas on October 11, 1983, constituting prohibited gifts under the Political Reform Act.
